author: @erictrician8853
text:
Interesting video,  you mentioned power cuts during the winter of discontent. 
The UK's organised power cuts happened during 1974 because of power rationing act of 1973. The winter of discontent was in 1978 , if there were any power cuts they were probably due to  the worst winter for 16 years.

author: @RahereTheGenius
text: The problems of large amounts of liquid came to a head with the Great London Beer Flood of 1814, when a brewery on fhe site of today's Dominion Theatre (where the second Star Wars  film premiered) saw it's entire brew, somethere between 1 and 3.5 million pints, disappear in the general direction of the River Thames, at the cost of 8 dead.

author: @markfuller6621
text: Believe the assets were bought up by Mercury Communications (part of Cable & Wireless) instant access into many buildings around The City for telecomms cables / fibre - the biggest cost for telcos is holes in the ground not the equipment!

author: @suzannehartmann946
text: New York City not only had pressurized water but steam for heating radiators throughout the city. "Beautification"  And "modernization" led to ugly wires on poles and coal smoke haze like in many other places.  Instead of your taxes providing water now you had separate bills for water and either for coal for a furnace or as the program progressed electric and indoor plumbing for additional services (toilets or as some say water closets).  Iceland of course still pumps preheated steam throughout courtesy of magma. Old black and white movies give a hint of what was used.

replies.author: @stevecummins324
replies.text:
I second such. 
+ I recon such might be a simple way of storing energy from offshore windfarms. Pump to circulate water which pulls the bubbles down to a large air bladder on sea floor. valve to let compressed out to drive a turbine etc. While such would be far from the most efficient as windturbines can be feathered so they don't turn in say high winds(and thus if rotated wouldn't "make wind" either) ... tip jets might be a very crude and fairly cheep way of testing the storage part of such an idea.

replies.author: @johnrhodez6829
replies.text:
The machenery under the stage of Drjry Lane theatre was operated by the London Hydraulic system.
Ideal of course as it would be very quiet. The equipment was still there unused when I visited the back stage area some years ago.
John Rhodes

author: @BertVerschuren
text: Call me dumb, but what exactly was it used for? Generating local power using pelton turbines?

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: I wondered this too! They usually used steam engines or water turbines 😁

replies.author: @stevecummins324
replies.text:
Hydraulics often used for goods and passenger lifts in buildings... Lot of people felt far safer in them than cable lifts. Plus no need for heavy winch gear at top. Only work well over a few floors. If buildings tended to get more floors, maybe why the decline too. 

Also Dock equipment.

author: @totherarf
text:
So a High Pressure Water company went into Liquidation? ....... I see what you did there! ;o)

Just as an aside Manchester had one too! In fact The Pump House fed water to power the lifts in the Town Hall (not next door) ..... and when the first electrical generating plant was fitted in the city it had to provide Steam for sale to the public!

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: It's amazing, they had a few set up around the UK. but all eventually disappeared.🙂

author: @spacecase0
text: Manufacturing moved out of London. Cities are good for getting a few hundred people together at the same time every day to build stuff. Hard to do that in the countryside. But when the cities get too expensive the manufacturing moves away. That high pressure water network disappeared because there weren't that many customers anymore. It was either low tech magazine or no Tech magazine has a fantastic article on that high pressure water system in London. You will have to search for it as I'm not allowed to put a web link here. And I always confuse those two magazines anyway. They've both got websites and are amazing.

replies.author: @stevecummins324
replies.text:
when manufacturing moved out... office work tended to move in. Hydraulic power more suited for driving ram type lifts than pelton wheels etc. and for less than a certain height ram lifts are better than cable driven lifts.
Once land values increased further, buildings started to be built higher,. cable lifts took over from hydraulic ram lifts.

author: @roberthopgood1894
text: Did I miss where you said WHY that company went into voluntary liquidation??

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text:
I think they started to decline as electric power started to become more popular.
But the system fully closed in 1977. The UK statutory authority, had the legal right to dig up the public highways to install and maintain its pipe network.
🤯

author: @paprjam
text: I visited this waterpower plant a few times. It's been under repair for the last few years so I don't know if it will be fired up anytime soon. They pump water from the Tennessee River up to the top of Racoon Mountain to use during high demand times. Racoon Moutain Pumped-Storage Plant in Tennessee. Wikipedia.
